title: Why Hycroft Mining Stock Sank 30% In March
content:
Key Points

Hycroft Mining stock fell along with gold and silver prices.  The company owns land that could be mined for gold and silver, but it generates no revenue right now.  The fact it doesn't have an operating business today should keep people away from the stock. 10 stocks we like better than Hycroft Mining ›

Shares of Hycroft Mining(NASDAQ: HYMC) fell 30.1% last month, according to data from S&P Global Market Intelligence. Investors shied away from speculative stocks amid global turmoil, with gold and silver prices falling significantly last month. Instead of a global safe haven, metals have been turned into speculative assets over the past year, and investors are now fleeing them.

Hycroft Mining is a prospective gold and silver miner that was benefiting from these rising prices, with its own shares up over 1,000% in the last twelve months. Now, the party has ended, at least for now. Here's why Hycroft Mining stock fell last month.

Will AI create the world's first trillionaire? Our team just released a report on the one little-known company, called an "Indispensable Monopoly" providing the critical technology Nvidia and Intel both need. Continue »

Following the price of gold and silver, not yet a safe haven

The price of gold hit $5,440 in late January. Silver almost reached $120 an ounce. Both were up significantly in the last year, with gold close to doubling and silver going from $30 an ounce to $120, a quadruple.

Since then, the last two months have been a poor period for metals investors. Silver is back down to $72, while gold has held up slightly better but is still down to around $4,650 an ounce.

As a prospective miner of gold and silver, Hycroft Mining stock collapsed along with these commodity prices last month. The company is a small outfit based in Nevada that is planning to start a mine in the coming years, and its stock price has exploded higher on momentum-driven metals trading over the last 12 months. Despite reports of better mineral deposits on the land it owns, Hycroft Mining has fallen quickly, breaking the momentum that took the stock up 10x in the past year.

Image source: Getty Images.

Should you buy Hycroft Mining stock?

Despite rising alongside the prices of gold and silver, Hycroft Mining does not actually conduct any mining operations today. It generated zero revenue in 2025 and burned $38 million in free cash flow. The business has never been profitable.

Sure, the company does have a lot of cash on the balance sheet, but that has come at shareholders' expense through common stock offerings, significantly increasing shares outstanding in recent years. Plus, mining operations require substantial upfront capital to get started, and it is unclear exactly how long it would take to get gold and silver to market.

Hycroft Mining's stock rose alongside the prices of gold and silver, but it was unable to capitalize on these higher prices by actually mining the metals. For this reason, investors should stay away from the stock at the moment.

Should you buy stock in Hycroft Mining right now?

Before you buy stock in Hycroft Mining, consider this:

The Motley Fool Stock Advisor analyst team just identified what they believe are the 10 best stocks for investors to buy now… and Hycroft Mining wasn’t one of them. The 10 stocks that made the cut could produce monster returns in the coming years.

Consider when Netflix made this list on December 17, 2004... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you’d have $532,066!* Or when Nvidia made this list on April 15, 2005... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you’d have $1,087,496!*

Now, it’s worth noting Stock Advisor’s total average return is 926% — a market-crushing outperformance compared to 185% for the S&P 500. Don't miss the latest top 10 list, available with Stock Advisor, and join an investing community built by individual investors for individual investors.

Brett Schafer has no position in any of the stocks mentioned. The Motley Fool has no position in any of the stocks mentioned. The Motley Fool has a disclosure policy.

title: Block sees lowest interest from short sellers in March among crypto firms with over $2B market cap
content:
Bitcoin (BTC-USD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BTC-USD]) prices continued to hover around the $60k to $70k level in March. The already volatile cryptocurrency was further pressured amid the U.S.-Iran conflict. While macroeconomic and geopolitical headwinds persist, diminishing optimism around U.S. crypto legislation has also eroded one of the market’s earlier tailwinds.

Among crypto stocks with a market cap of more than $2B, application software firms witnessed the highest short interest in the previous month. CleanSpark (CLSK [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/CLSK]) continued to lead the list with short interest rising to 33.08%, followed by MARA Holdings (MARA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/MARA]), which saw SI rise to 30.08% in the month.

title: Why Shares of Hycroft Mining Stock Sank 18% This Week
content:
Shares of Hycroft Mining(NASDAQ: HYMC) have slipped 18% so far this week, according to data from S&P Global Market Intelligence. The prospective gold and silver miner is falling because the prices of both metals have fallen during the market uproar over the conflict between the United States, Israel, and Iran.

After zooming to start the year, Hycroft Mining stock is down 44% from its highs. Here's why it was falling yet again, and whether now is a good time to buy the dip on the stock.

Will AI create the world's first trillionaire? Our team just released a report on the one little-known company, called an "Indispensable Monopoly" providing the critical technology Nvidia and Intel both need. Continue »

Following metals prices

Hycroft Mining owns a prospective gold and silver mine in Nevada. The company is currently not operating its mine but is exploring the potential resources it has, with updates this year highlighting larger deposits than it originally believed it had.

Along with gold and silver prices rocketing higher, Hycroft Mining became one of the top-performing stocks worldwide, with shares at one point up 1,000% over the last 12 months. Now, with silver almost cut in half and gold down from $5,500 to around $4,500, investors have lost their love for mining stocks.Image source: Getty Images.

Should you buy the dip?

At the end of the day, mining stocks are going to follow the price of the metals or commodities they mine, and Hycroft Mining is no exception.

What's unfortunate is that the company cannot capitalize on these elevated gold and silver prices because it does not currently have an operating mine. It may not have one for many years, and is not guiding for any production in 2026. This makes the stock incredibly risky, even though it is almost down 50% from its highs. Avoid buying this pre-revenue mining stock for your portfolio right now.

Should you buy stock in Hycroft Mining right now?

Before you buy stock in Hycroft Mining, consider this:

The Motley Fool Stock Advisor analyst team just identified what they believe are the 10 best stocks for investors to buy now… and Hycroft Mining wasn’t one of them. The 10 stocks that made the cut could produce monster returns in the coming years.

Consider when Netflix made this list on December 17, 2004... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you’d have $494,747!* Or when Nvidia made this list on April 15, 2005... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you’d have $1,094,668!*

title: Thursday's ETF Movers: FCG, SLVR
content:
In trading on Thursday, the First Trust Natural Gas ETF is outperforming other ETFs, up about 3.1% on the day.  Components of that ETF showing particular strength include shares of Vermilion Energy, up about 15.9% and shares of Woodside Energy Group, up about 9.6% on the day.

And underperforming other ETFs today is the Sprott Silver Miners & Physical Silver ETF, off about 7.5% in Thursday afternoon trading. Among components of that ETF with the weakest showing on Thursday were shares of Hycroft Mining Holding, lower by about 12.8%, and shares of Avino Silver & Gold Mines, lower by about 11.8% on the day.

VIDEO: Thursday's ETF Movers: FCG, SLVR

title: Thursday Sector Laggards: Precious Metals, Hospital & Medical Practitioners
content:
In trading on Thursday, precious metals shares were relative laggards, down on the day by about 7.3%.  Helping drag down the group were shares of Hycroft Mining Holding, down about 12.6% and shares of Avino Silver & Gold Mines off about 12% on the day.

Also lagging the market Thursday are hospital & medical practitioners shares, down on the day by about 3.2% as a group, led down by Agilon Health, trading lower by about 23.2% and AirSculpt Technologies, trading lower by about 11.1%.

title: UK borrowing costs rise three times faster than rest of Europe
content:
An explosion overnight near an Aramco oil facility in Riyadh - Social Media via REUTERS

UK borrowing costs have jumped three times faster than other major European economies as the Iran war threatened to trigger an energy price shock.

The cost of short-term government borrowing surged at its fastest pace in nearly two years after Iran launched missile strikes on energy plants in the Middle East.

Oil and gas prices soared after the attacks, threatening to drive up inflation across the global economy.

Central banks abandoned plans to cut interest rates to stimulate growth as a result.

Andrew Bailey, the Governor of the Bank of England, said he stood “ready to act” to bring down inflation after policymakers held interest rates at 3.75pc on Thursday.

The decision led traders to ramp up bets on rate rises this year, at one point pricing in interest rates of 4.5pc before the end of 2026.

James Lynch, an investment manager at Aegon Asset Management, said bond traders were “bracing for potential rate hikes”.

The yield on two-year gilts – the return the Treasury promises to holders of Britain’s £2.9tn national debt – rocketed from 4.1pc to as high as 4.49pc in its steepest rise since August 2024.

UK bond yields rose at a far more rapid pace than European rivals.

Pierre Roke, an analyst at Validus Risk Management, said UK bonds had been hit hard because Britain “is in a notably worse position to provide fiscal support”.

08:20pm

Signing off...

That’s all from us today but we will be back tomorrow with all the latest market reaction to the war. In the meantime you can read our business and economics news here.

08:18pm

Markets wrap

US stocks fell as the energy crisis continued to rock markets on the 20th day of the war.

The S&P 500 fell by 0.27pc while the Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped by 0.44pc and the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite fell by 0.28pc.

Metals producers were the biggest fallers on the stock market after gold dropped by 4.94pc and aluminium fell by more than 5pc.

Shares in US gold and silver mining company Hycroft fell by 12.4pc while Century Aluminium Company plunged by 10.3pc.

07:59pm

Interest rate rise increasingly likely

Pantheon Macroeconomics said it will start forecasting a UK interest rate rise if energy prices stay at current levels for another three days.

The consultancy’s chief UK economist Rob Wood said: “Futures markets are pricing energy costs close to the level we think warrants an increase to Bank Rate, and the MPC’s language suggests the bar to a hike is lower than previously thought.

“So, we will likely add a hike to our forecast in H2 2026, if energy futures curves hold at yesterday’s levels, or higher, over the weekend.”

Story Continues

07:53pm

Oil prices dip

The price of Brent crude has fallen to $107 per barrel, down from the peak of $117 that it hit in early trading today.

However, this is still up 47pc since the start of the war.

The gap between Brent crude and West Texas Intermediate (WTI) narrowed after the White House made it clear the US was not planning to introduce oil export curbs.

WTI is currently trading at $94 per barrel, $12 less than crude. Earlier in the day, the gap widened to as much as $20 on fears that America would restrict exports.

Before the war began, the gap was typically $3.

07:47pm

$200bn war cash plea

Donald Trump will ask Congress for an extra $200bn to cover the costs of the war in Iran.

The president told reporters the money will be a “small price” to pay for the war.

Mr Trump said: “We want to be in the best shape, the best shape we’ve ever been in. It’s a small price to pay to make sure that we stay tippy top.”

This sum will be in addition to the defence department’s annual budget of $838.7bn, which Congress approved in January.

But the request is already being opposed by US politicians.

Democratic Senator Chris Van Hollen said on X. “The best way to end this war, protect our ​troops, save civilian lives, and rein in a lawless ​Administration is to cut off funding. I’m a hell no.”

07:00pm

US government borrowing costs jump

US government borrowing costs surged on Thursday as fears mount over a new inflation wave.

Yields on two-year Treasuries jumped by nine basis points to 3.84pc, their highest level since August.

Rates on 10-year notes rose by two basis points to a two-month high of 4.28pc.

06:55pm

Risk of long-term supply shock

Gulf nations could grapple with major production losses for years after the conflict ends, Goldman Sachs has warned.

Oil analyst Daan Struyven said that oil shocks typically result in “persistent supply losses” due to damage to infrastructure such as export terminals and low investment.

Across the five major oil supply shocks over the last 50 years, the average blow to a country’s oil production four years later was 42pc, the investment bank found.

Four years after the wars in 1980 between Iran and Iraq, both countries’ production was down by 64pc.

Mr Struyven said: “Persistent production losses in Iran or especially the broader Middle East could be very significant because Iran and the 7 other Persian Gulf countries produced 3.5mb/d of crude (4pc of global crude production) and 21.8mb/d (26pc), respectively, in 2025.”

Goldman’s base case scenario is currently for production to normalise four weeks after the Strait of Hormuz reopens, but Mr Struyven warned: “we see meaningful downside risk to long-term supply.”

06:40pm

Plans to evacuate ships from Gulf

The UN’s shipping agency will try to establish a maritime corridor to evacuate commercial ships from the Gulf.

The International Maritime Organisation (IMO)’s governing council has agreed to set up a framework to enable trapped ships to journey out through Iran’s blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

Around 2,000 ships and 20,000 seafarers are stuck west of the Strait of Hormuz.

The IMO has not given a timeline for when it expected to be able to set up a corridor for safe passage out of the strait.

IMO Secretary General Arsenio Dominguez called for ships stationed east of the strait to avoid taking the “unnecessary risk” of sailing through it.

He warned: “We must not expose seafarers to a higher risk than they already face right now.”

06:31pm

No US export restrictions

The US energy secretary has confirmed that the US does not intend to restrict its oil and gas exports.

Chris Wright said on X: “To be clear, the Trump administration has no plan to implement restrictions on oil and gas exports.”

05:47pm

FTSE-100 down 2.35pc

UK stocks tumbled on Thursday as companies were hammered by higher energy prices.

The FTSE-100 closed down by 2.35pc as banks and housebuilders fell sharply.

Barratt Redrow, Britain’s biggest housebuilder, saw the steepest drop in the index and was down by 8.45pc on fears that higher mortgage rates will depress demand for new homes.

Persimmon, another major builder, was also down by 5.95pc.

NatWest saw the second largest fall in the index, dropping by 8pc.

Rolls Royce, which makes jet engines, slumped by 5.22pc while EasyJet was down by 4.68pc as the energy crisis hits the airline industry.

04:54pm

No UK rate cuts this year

The Bank of England will not cut interest rates this year, Deutsche Bank has warned.

The investment bank’s chief UK economist Sanjay Raja said: “We are changing our Bank of England call. We no longer expect any rate cuts this year. We expect Bank Rate to stay put at 3.75pc for the remainder of the year.

The prospect of an interest rate rise is also becoming more likely.

Mr Raja added: “The bar for rate hikes, we think, has fallen meaningfully – contrary to our previous view.

“What will it take for the MPC to hike rates? One, limited fiscal support to curb inflation. And two, duration of the Iran conflict lasting into April (and beyond), extending the length of the inflation shock.

“Should fears of second-round effects ratchet higher, the case for a policy pivot in Q2-26 will likely strengthen. We will be monitoring this closely.”

04:50pm

Dubai crude surpasses $170

The price of Dubai crude oil has surged to an unprecedented $170 per barrel.

This means the price has climbed by 143pc since the conflict in the Middle East began. Before the war, it was around $70 per barrel.

Oil researcher Rory Johnston said on X: “To my knowledge, no crude has ever commanded more than $170/bbl before.”

04:30pm

Trump ‘not considering’ oil export ban

The White House is not considering banning oil exports as the war in Iran sends global prices soaring.

An official told the Financial Times: “Oil and gas export restrictions are not under consideration.”

Energy bosses are preparing to meet US vice president JD Vance to discuss measures to alleviate the energy crisis for US households.

03:59pm

Oil prices hit $111

The price of Brent crude jumped by another 3.7pc to hit $111.5 per barrel on Thursday.

Oil prices are now up 53pc since the conflict began at the end of February and 84pc since the start of this year.

03:54pm

US petrol prices surge by a third

Petrol pump prices in America have surged by 33pc in the past month as the war in Iran sends oil prices soaring.

Average US pump prices jumped to $3.88 per gallon on Thursday, up from $2.93 a month ago, according to the AAA.

California has the highest pump prices in the country, at $5.62 per gallon, followed by Washington at $5.14.

The price surge follows Donald Trump’s repeated insistence in recent months that gas prices would soon fall to $2 per gallon.

03:22pm

Several rate rises ‘unlikely’, say bond traders

The Bank of England is “unlikely” to raise interest rates several times this year despite the war in the Middle East putting Britain on course for an inflation shock, bond traders have said.

The weakness in Britain’s jobs market will prevent the series of jumps being priced on money markets, according to Jason Borbora-Sheen, income portfolio manager at Ninety One.

Official figures on Thursday showed that unemployment remained at a five-year high of 5.2pc in the three months to January, while wage growth dropped to a five-year low.

Mr Borbora-Sheen said bond yields were being pushed higher as a result of the unanimous vote by the Bank of England to keep rates at 3.75pc, as well as Governor Andrew Bailey’s language that he “stands ready to act as is necessary” to keep inflation down.

Mr Borbora-Sheen said this was “likely a response to criticisms of their reaction function in 2022”, when the Bank was accused of failing to raise interest rates quickly enough in response to the inflation shock caused by Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.

He added: “Nevertheless, we see a succession of rate hikes very unlikely, given the weakness in labour markets witnessed prior to conflict in the Middle East.”

Other traders were happy to join in the large scale dumping of UK bonds.

Daniele Antonucci, chief investment officer at Quintet Private Bank, said: “Given inflation concerns, and some degree of political uncertainty, we recently sold some gilts.”

03:19pm

German bond yields soar

Government borrowing costs are soaring across Europe as the energy crisis brings fears of a new inflation wave.

Yields on German two-year government bonds jumped by 18 basis points on Thursday to 2.62pc after the European Central Bank warned the war in the Middle East would push up prices across the economy in the near term.

Yields on 10-year German bonds, which are the benchmark for the eurozone, rose by 7bps to 3.01pc.

03:10pm

Disappearing mortgage deals

More mortgages will disappear in the coming days as soaring bond yields force lenders to reprice loans, brokers warn.

Nicholas Mendes, of John Charcol, said: “If energy prices remain elevated and inflation expectations continue to rise, the risk is no longer just that rate cuts are delayed, but that the Bank may need to consider raising rates again.

“For mortgage borrowers, the effect is immediate. Fixed rates are driven by future funding costs rather than today’s Bank Rate alone, so lenders do not need to wait for an actual base rate rise before withdrawing products or increasing pricing.

“I would expect more short-notice rate withdrawals over the coming days, and potentially some lenders temporarily stepping back while markets remain this unsettled.”

03:00pm

Bond market ‘bracing for interest rate rises’

James Lynch, an investment manager at Aegon Asset Management, said bond traders were “bracing itself for potential rate hikes”.

The yield on two-year gilts – the return the Treasury promises to holders of Britain’s £2.9 trillion national debt – rocketed from 4.1pc to as high as 4.49pc in its steepest rise since August 2024.

Mr Lynch added that borrowing costs could rise further if inflation fears grow, as well as over “concerns about the UK’s worsening fiscal situation – higher debt interest costs, lower tax take and potential subsidies”.

02:34pm

Bank of England leans ‘more towards rate hikes rather than cuts’

The Bank of England has shown it is more concerned about anything that may drive up inflation rather than any potential blow to growth from higher borrowing costs, economists said.

Paul Dales, chief UK economist at Capital Economics, said the Monetary Policy Committee had given the impression that it “has its finger hovering over the interest rate hike button”.

Policymakers said they “stand ready” to take action if needed. Mr Dales said on the previous four periods when the MPC has used the phrase “stands ready”, on two occasions they took action within four months.

Having said in February that it expected inflation to fall to “around 2pc”, the Bank now forecasts it will be “between 3pc and 3.5pc over the next couple of quarters”.

02:21pm

Mortgage rates ramped up after Bank of England decision

Lenders have already pushed up mortgage rates after the Bank of England signalled that it “stands ready” to raise borrowing costs if the Iran war triggers an inflation shock.

Halifax said it will put up rates by 0.1pc on all fixed rate products for buyers, and by 0.15pc for those seeking to remortgage.

The changes will come into effect from Friday.

02:03pm

Qatar gas plant to take 3-5 years to repair after Iranian strikes

The boss of QatarEnergy said Iran’s attacks on its facilities in the Gulf will take three to five years to repair.

In a blow to global energy markets, Saad al-Kaabi told Reuters that the strikes had damaged facilities that produce 17pc ​of the company’s ​liquefied natural gas export capacity.

He said: “I never in my wildest ‌dreams ⁠would have thought that Qatar would be – Qatar and the region – in ​such ​an ⁠attack, especially from a brotherly Muslim ​country in the month ​of ⁠Ramadan, attacking us in this way.”

Gas prices have soared in the wake of the attacks overnight. Europe’s benchmark contract was last up 17pc on Thursday.

01:54pm

Bailey urges traders against ‘reaching any strong conclusions’ on rates

Andrew Bailey cautioned markets about “reaching any strong conclusions” about interest rates as traders rapidly increased bets on higher borrowing costs this year.

Money markets briefly priced in three increases in interest rates this year after the Governor of the Bank of England said he “stands ready to act” to any inflationary shock caused by conflict in the Middle East.

In an apparent attempt to ease the market turmoil, Mr Bailey told broadcasters: “We will have to judge these things. That’s why I caution against reaching any strong conclusions about us raising interest rates.

“We will have to take those decisions. But today, we’ve given a very clear message, the right place to be is on hold.”

He said the “best and most appropriate solution” to the turmoil was the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, where a fifth of the world’s oil and gas exports usually pass. It has been effectively closed by the Iran war.

Mr Bailey said: “We do this against a very different context to the one we had three or four years ago. We’ve got higher interest rates, and we’ve got demand in the economy, which has been relatively soft.”

01:46pm

US stocks fall as Iran war intensifies

Wall ⁠Street’s main indexes opened lower as the Iran war sent oil prices soaring.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped by 0.6pc to 45,967.88 as the Bank of England and the European Central Bank followed the Federal Reserve in taking a more cautious stance on interest rates.

The S&P 500 fell 0.7pc to 6,579.29 while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite declined by 0.9pc to 21,951.26.

01:33pm

Borrowing cost surge as Bailey hints at rate rises

The cost of short-term government borrowing has surged at its fastest pace in nearly two years after Andrew Bailey signalled the Bank of England was ready to raise interest rates.

The yield on two-year gilts – the return the Treasury promises to pay buyers of its debt – rocketed by 39 basis points to 4.49pc on Thursday in its steepest rise since August 2024.

It came as the Governor of the Bank of England said he was “ready to act as necessary” to keep inflation down after policymakers voted unanimously to leave interest rates at 3.75pc.

Pierre Roke, an analyst at Validus Risk Management, said UK bonds had been hit hard because Britain “is in a notably worst position to provide fiscal support”.

“This helps explain why UK rates have seen one of the more pronounced repricing across the G10, reflecting the economy’s sensitivity to energy-driven inflation shocks.”

01:17pm

ECB leaves interest rates at 2pc

The European Central Bank voted to keep interest rates at 2pc as the Iran war continued to trigger turmoil in markets.

The Governing Council said: “The war in the Middle East has made the outlook significantly more uncertain, creating upside risks for inflation and downside risks for economic growth.

“It will have a material impact on near-term inflation through higher energy prices. Its medium-term implications will depend both on the intensity and duration of the conflict and on how energy prices affect consumer prices and the economy.”

01:16pm

Bessent denies US intervention in oil market

The US treasury secretary said there had been no government intervention in the oil trading market after suggestions a move was under consideration.

Last week, Doug Burgum, the US interior secretary, said officials had discussed trading in the so-called futures markets after war in Iran saw the price of crude surge.

Terry Duffy, the CME Group chief executive, said government intervention risked a “biblical disaster”.

In an interview with Fox Business, Scott Bessent said the US Treasury was not intervening in the futures market.

12:51pm

Memories of 2022 ‘risks worsening inflation shock’

The Bank of England has warned that any inflationary shock resulting from the ongoing conflict in the Middle East risks being worsened by sensitive households and businesses.

In the minutes of today’s Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) meeting, policymakers said they were alert to the risk that an inflation spike could be worsened as rising energy costs give way to increased wage and price demands.

This would be compounded by households and businesses engaging in “self-perpetuating” behaviours that could “embed domestic inflationary pressures”, owing to their heightened sensitivity as a result of “successive negative supply shocks in recent times”.

The MPC was also concerned that the economy was in a much worse state today than it was in 2022, meaning there is little to no buffer left for households or businesses to absorb higher costs.

This could hammer economic confidence and cause people to engage in “precautionary saving”, which would further hamper demand.

All of this could result in “a more rapid or larger rise in unemployment”, which is already headed towards a five-year high.

12:46pm

US may ‘unsanction’ Iranian oil, says Bessent

Scott Bessent said the US may remove sanctions from Iranian oil on stranded tankers to help ease the squeeze on global supplies caused by Donald Trump’s war in the Middle East.

The US treasury secretary told ​Fox Business: “In ​the coming ‌days, we may unsanction the ‌Iranian oil that’s ​on ⁠the water. It’s ⁠about 140 ​million barrels.”

12:24pm

Traders bet on at least two rate rises this year

Traders dramatically ramped up bets on the Bank of England raising interest rates this year after the Governor signalled he was “ready to act” to keep inflation down.

Money markets are pricing in two rate rises this year, compared to one before the decision, with a 50pc chance that borrowing costs could be pushed up three times in the wake of the Iran war.

Seema Shah, an analyst at Principal Asset Management, said that even though rates were kept on hold at 3.75pc, the Bank of England sounded much more ready to raise rates.

She said the backing of Swati Dhingra, who has repeatedly voted for rate cuts, “speaks volumes about the depth of the Bank’s inflation discomfort”.

She said: “While the labour market would benefit from near‑term easing, persistently sticky inflation – and the renewed uncertainty stemming from the Middle East conflict – has effectively taken that option off the table for now.”

12:14pm

Bailey signals he is ready to raise rates

The Governor of the Bank of England signalled he is ready to increase interest rates in the face of the energy shock caused by the Iran war.

Andrew Bailey said he would be “monitoring developments extremely closely and stand ready to act as necessary” to make sure inflation remains at the 2pc target.

His colleague Swati Dhingra, who has repeatedly voted for rate cuts, went a step further and said that war could “warrant a hold or increase in Bank Rate” in the event of “severe and longer-lasting constraints on oil and gas supply”.

12:10pm

Pound rises as policymakers warn of higher inflation

The value of the pound rose as the Bank of England warned inflation would be “higher in the near term” due to the conflict in the Middle East.

Sterling was up 0.3pc against the dollar at $1.33 and rose by 0.1pc versus the euro, which was worth 86.3p.

Andrew Bailey, Governor of the Bank of the England said: “War in the Middle East has pushed up global energy prices.

“You can already see that at the petrol pump and, if it lasts, it will feed into higher household energy bills later in the year.

“The best way to tackle this is at the source by re-opening energy supply lines.

“We have held interest rates at 3.75pc as we assess how events unfold.  Whatever happens, our job is to make sure inflation gets back to its 2pc target.”

12:05pm

Inflation will be higher in the near term, warns Bank of England

Policymakers at the Bank of England voted unanimously to keep interest rates on hold in the face of the Iran war.

The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) said: “Conflict in the Middle East has caused a significant increase in global energy and other commodity prices, which will affect households’ fuel and utility prices and have indirect effects via businesses’ costs.

“Prior to this, there had been continued disinflation in domestic prices and wages. CPI inflation will be higher in the near term as a result of the new shock to the economy.

“Monetary policy cannot influence global energy prices but aims to ensure that the economic adjustment to them occurs in a way that achieves the 2pc target sustainably.

“The MPC is alert to the increased risk of domestic inflationary pressures through second-round effects in wage and price-setting, the risk of which will be greater the longer higher energy prices persist.

“The MPC is also assessing the implications for inflation of the weakening in economic activity that is likely to result from higher energy costs.

“The Committee will continue to monitor closely the situation in the Middle East and its impact on global energy supply and energy prices. It stands ready to act as necessary to ensure that CPI inflation remains on track to meet the 2pc target in the medium term.”

12:00pm

Bank of England holds interest rates at 3.75pc

The Bank of England has kept interest rates on hold at 3.75pc as it braces for an inflation shock triggered by the war in Iran.

Members of the Bank’s Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) voted to leave borrowing costs unchanged on Thursday, just as conflict in the Middle East sent oil and gas prices soaring.

Before the war started at the end of February, investors had been expecting the Bank to cut interest rates to 3.5pc to support the economy as growth stalled and unemployment jumped.

11:38am

Borrowing costs soar after missile strikes

The cost of government borrowing leapt again after the latest missile strikes, which have raised the risk of a global inflation shock.

Bond yields have soared even as the Bank of England and European Central Bank (ECB) are both expected to keep interest rates on hold in the face of the intensifying Middle East conflict.

The yield on 10-year UK gilts, the return the Treasury offers to buyers of its debt, has jumped from 4.73pc to 4.83pc and is up from 4.23pc before the war began.

The yield on two-year UK bonds, which are more sensitive to interest rates, was up from 4.1pc to 4.24pc on Thursday in its biggest jump since October 2024.

Euro zone government bond yields also rose, with Germany’s two-year yield up nearly eight basis points to 2.52pc, the highest since August 2024.

11:13am

Gold plunges as traders bet on fewer rate cuts

The price of gold slumped further as the war in Iran made it less likely that central banks will cut interest rates.

Bullion fell as much as 4.6pc to $4,667 per ounce as the soaring price of oil and gas raised the prospect of a global inflation shock.

Gold has come under pressure from multiple angles since the start of the war in the Middle East, having hit record highs above $5,335 in January.

It has fallen 10pc since the conflict began as investors have turned to the safe haven US dollar during the conflict, which has made gold more expensive.

As traders bet that interest rates will stay higher for longer, it encourages investors to move money into assets that pay a return, like government bonds, rather than gold, which offers to returns.

The turmoil has also hit silver, which has dropped by 24pc during the conflict.

Nick Cawley, an analyst at Solomon Global, said: “With fewer cuts on the horizon and a resilient dollar, the near-term outlook for gold and silver looks challenging.”

10:51am

Shell gas site damaged in Iran attacks

Shell said there had been damage caused to one of its gas plants in Qatar in the Iranian missile attacks.

The FTSE 100 energy giant’s share price was down 0.4pc despite surging prices for oil and gas since the strikes overnight.

The company said the Iranian missile attacks had “caused damage” to its Pearl “gas-to-liquids” facility at the Ras Laffan field.

A spokesman said: “We put our emergency response into action, all essential staff who were on site are accounted for and no injuries were reported.

“The fire was quickly put out and Pearl is now in a safe state.

“We are working in close co-ordination with the Qatari authorities and our partners at Qatar Energy to manage the situation and to assess the damage.”

10:41am

FTSE 100 plunges over inflation fears

The FTSE 100 plummeted as the missile strikes in the Middle East threatened to derail global growth.

The UK’s flagship index was down 1.9pc, with the mid-cap FTSE 250 dropping by 2pc to its lowest ⁠level since November last year.

Metal miners were the worst performers as the price of gold and silver fell, with banks collectively down 3.7pc.

It comes as traders have ramped up bets on higher interest rates, betting that the Bank of England will raise borrowing costs by July to battle an inflation shock. Higher borrowing costs tend to weigh down on growth.

Gary Smith of Columbia Threadneedle Investments said: “With the economy already showing signs of fragility, notably in labour market indicators, an energy shock risks adding to existing pressures.”

10:16am

Saudi oil refinery hit in drone attack

Saudi Arabia said its Samref oil refinery has been hit in a drone attack.

The site in Yanbu, which is run by state oil giant Aramco and Exxon, is close to the oil export terminal in the west of the country.

Pipelines have been carrying crude across the country to the site to bypass the Strait of Hormuz, which has been effectively closed since the start of the conflict.

10:10am

‘If only we were an island surrounded by oil and gas’

Britain is “paying the price of globalisation” by relying on other countries for its energy needs, Telegraph readers have said.

Here are a selection of views from the comments section below as oil and gas prices soar. You can join the debate too.

09:52am

Oil surges by 10pc after missile strikes

The price of oil surged further following the missile strikes across the Middle East.

Brent crude, the international benchmark, was up as much as 10.9pc to $119 a barrel.

US-produced West Texas Intermediate climbed at a slower rate, up as much as 3.8pc to $100.

Robin Brooks, an economist at the Brookings Institution, said: “Brent – at current levels – is pricing very material supply disruption, so there’s limited scope for prices to go higher.”

He added: “As oil prices rise, the risk of a Trump Taco goes up. As the US and Israel continue to pound Iran, the risk that the regime falls must also be rising. There’s lots of catalysts for prices to fall, which gets forgotten amid the ‘fog of war’.”

09:30am

Inflation to pass 4pc in gas shock, warn economists

Inflation will surge back up to 4pc later this year as surging oil and gas prices push up the cost of fuel and electricity, economists have warned.

Pantheon Macroeconomics said the pace of price rises would only fall slightly from the current level of 3pc to 2.8pc in April as a result of the jump in energy prices.

Inflaton will then hit 3.9pc in August and 4pc in November, they said, underlining the challenge faced by the Bank of England as it announces its next decision on interest rates later.

Europe’s benchmark gas contract was last up 23pc on Thursday, while oil was up nearly 8pc to just under $116 a barrel after Iran launched strikes on energy infrastructure in Qatar and Saudi Arabia.

Chief UK economist Rob Wood said: “We are beginning to add second-round effects to our inflation forecasts now, because inflation would on current energy prices reach the psychologically important 4pc level, which research finds leads households to raise their inflation expectations more aggressively.

“Those second-round effects prolong the likely period of above-target inflation.

“These forecasts are sensitive to the duration of the energy price shock. The numbers here take current futures curves at face value, but a quick de-escalation of threats over gas infrastructure would likely mean a sharp unwind of the gas price spike this morning and therefore pull our inflation projections back down.

“Of course, the opposite is true too. Uncertainty is very high and as we all know, intra-day volatility is high too.”

09:08am

We’re worried about energy prices, says minister

A trade minister has admitted he is “worried” about the recent surge in oil and gas prices during the Iran war.

Chris Bryant told Times Radio: “We’re worried in the UK [about] energy prices. Now we are well supplied, we’re watching it, we’ve had a series of meetings.

“We need to be less reliant in the future on oil and gas. That’s why it’s so important for the UK that we develop a strategy for the UK to supply as much of our renewable energy as we possibly can.”

09:01am

Bills to be £300 higher for at least a year says biggest electricity generator

Household energy bills will be up to £300 higher for at least the next year, Britain’s biggest power generator has suggested.

EDF said it expected Ofgem’s annualised price cap to rise to more than £1,900 through this autumn and winter, following the oil chaos in the Middle East.

The cap – which is the average annual energy bill faced by a family home – is set at £1,641 for April to July.Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, and Martin McCluskey, the energy minister, met consumer groups and charities on Wednesday at a roundtable event to discuss energy costs - Shaun Curry / DESNZ

08:45am

Traders bet on rate rise as gas prices soar

The Bank of England will raise interest rates by September, money markets show after the latest surge in oil and gas prices.

Traders are pricing in an increase in rates from 3.75pc to 4pc later this year, with a 47pc chance of a second jump before the end of 2026.

It comes as soaring oil and gas prices threaten to push up inflation, with ING warning the consumer prices index will climb to 3.5pc in the third quarter of this year.

Europe’s benchmark gas contract was last up 21pc while UK natural gas prices also surged by 21pc to 168p per therm.

It follows Iranian strikes on the world’s largest LNG production facility at Ras Laffan in Qatar, which followed Israeli strikes on its South Pars gas field on Wednesday.Smoke and fire rise near the South Pars gas field following an attack on Wednesday - via REUTERS

08:17am

Gas plant strikes ‘could be a game changer’

The strikes on the massive Ras Laffan LNG facility could be a “game changer” similar to the sabotage of the Nord Stream pipeline, analysts have warned.

The pipeline, which for years had shipped Russian gas under the Baltic Sea to Europe, was damaged by huge blasts, just months after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in February 2022.

Qatar, a key source of natural gas for world markets, said firefighters put out a blaze at its major Ras Laffan LNG facility after it was hit by Iranian missile attacks.

Production has already been halted there after earlier attacks but it said the latest wave of missiles caused “sizeable fires and extensive further damage”.

“This could be a game changer for the LNG industry, akin to the attack on Nord Stream or possibly even worse,” Susan Sakmar of University of Houston Law Center told Bloomberg News.

“This is a sudden disruption, with no indication that Qatar could restart anytime soon.”

08:05am

Stocks plunge after oil and gas shock

The FTSE 100 plunged at the start of trading after the intensification of the Iran war sent oil and gas prices surging.

The UK’s flagship stock index dropped by 1.1pc to 10,195.20 while the mid-cap FTSE 250 declined by 1.2pc to 21,826.69.

08:03am

Inflation ‘to hit 3.5pc’ after Iran war shock

Inflation in Britain will reach 3.5pc in the third quarter of this year asthe Iran war pushes up energy prices and squeezes the supply of goods, ING has warned.

The Dutch bank has revised its forecasts in light of the conflict, which it said would be a “supply-side shock, pushing up inflation and posing new challenges for central banks”.

Inflation in Britain will only fall to 2.4pc in the second quarter from 3pc in the first three months of the year, it said.

From then, it forecast that the consumer prices index (CPI) will rise to 3.5pc in the third quarter and then ease to 3.4pc in final three months of 2026.

It will not drop back close to the Bank of England’s 2pc target until the fourth quarter of 2027 at 2.1pc, it warned.

07:47am

Bank of England poised to hold rates over inflation fears

The surge in gas prices underlines the challenge facing the Bank of England as it prepares to announce its next decision on interest rates.

Members of the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) are expected to keep interest rates unchanged at 3.75pc at noon as the Iran war risks driving up inflation with higher energy prices.

Some economists and markets had been expecting policymakers to deliver a cut, prior to the escalation of the war in the Middle East.

Edward Allenby, senior UK economist for Oxford Economics, said: “The UK inflation outlook was starting to brighten, but the conflict in the Middle East has thrown a spanner in the works.

“Against this backdrop, it’s almost certain that the MPC will keep bank rate unchanged at 3.75pc at the March meeting.

“If the shock proves short-lived and recent price rises fully reverse, we still think there’s a reasonable chance that the MPC will resume its cutting cycle either in April or June.

“However, if the surge in energy prices persists or goes higher, the MPC will be set for an extended pause.”

07:40am

Gas prices soar at fastest pace since first days of war

The price of gas rocketed at its fastest pace since the first two days of trading after the start of the war in the Middle East.

The jump of 35pc after European markets opened was the steepest intraday jumps since the Monday and Tuesday after the US and Israel launched the strikes that killed Iran’s former Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei.

On those days, surges of 54pc and 48pc were recorded, before closing up 39pc and 22pc, respectively.Iran attacked Qatar’s Ras Laffan gas facility overnight

07:32am

Oil surges after highest close in four years

Oil prices continued to climb after Donald Trump threatened to “massively blow up” Iran’s South Pars natural gas field after Iranian missiles hit Qatar.

Brent crude reached its highest closing level since July 2022 overnight at $107.38 per barrel.

The price was up a further 7pc on Thursday to nearly $115 a barrel after the US president’s threats.

Iran overnight twice struck Ras Laffan – the world’s largest LNG export facility.

President Trump posted late last night that the US knew nothing of Israel’s attack against the South Pars gas field.

He added that “no more attacks will be made by Israel” against South Pars if Iran stops its own strikes.

Jim Reid, an analyst at Deutsche Bank said: “Despite those comments, oil markets remain on edge in Asia this morning amid fears that energy infrastructure could be meaningfully damaged.”

07:17am

Good morning

Thanks for joining me. Gas prices surged by 35pc after Iran launched strikes on the world’s biggest LNG plant.

Europe’s benchmark gas contract leapt to €74 per megawatt hour on Thursday, more than double its levels from before the Middle East conflict.

The price of Brent crude oil also surged by 7pc to nearly $115 a barrel after Iran escalated its retaliatory attacks in the Middle East.

Qatar said that the Ras Laffan liquefied natural gas (LNG) export plant suffered “extensive damage” in a series of attacks last night.

Donald Trump said he would “massively blow up” Iran’s strategic South Pars gas field if it attacked other nations in the Gulf again. Here is what you need to know.

5 things to start your day

UK hits Chinese steel with 50pc tariffs to protect British industry | Peter Kyle confirms tax-free imports to be cut by three fifths in effort to shield domestic production Fed raises inflation forecasts after Trump’s war triggers ‘oil shock’ | US interest rates held as soaring energy costs intensify fears of a new wave of rising prices AI becomes a battleground between tech bros and the masses | Politicians’ enthusiasm for data centres faces public resistance as fears over job security grows Rolls-Royce scraps electric car target as ‘drivers prefer V12 engines’ | Luxury marque will continue making petrol cars into the next decade after abandoning 2030 all-electric goal Attack on Gail’s reopens anti-Semitism wounds at The Guardian | Protests at newspaper’s HQ amid claims of anti-Semitism in op-ed about vandalism of bakery

What happened overnight

Stocks slid and oil prices rose sharply after a major escalation in the Iran.

Donald Trump said an angry Israel “violently lashed out” and attacked Iran’s major South Pars gas field, prompting Iran to fire missiles at Qatar and Saudi Arabia.

The US president said he would “massively blow up” the South Pars field if Iran targeted Qatar again.

Brent crude oil rose more than 5pc to more than $113 a barrel and natural gas prices jumped 3pc.

In stocks, Japan’s Nikkei was down over 3pc, while South Korea’s benchmark index fell 2.8pc.

MSCI’s broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan fell more than 2.5pc. European stock indexes were on track to open down more than 1.5pc.

The dollar index, which measures the US currency against six other units, is up 2.5pc since the war broke out at the end of February as investors look for safe havens. The index was last at 100.15, slightly lower after a 0.7pc rise on Wednesday.

Stocks on Wall Street ended the day lower amid fresh worries about escalating violence in the Middle East and the Federal Reserve’s decision to keep interest rates on hold.

Attacks on energy facilities across the Gulf region pushed oil prices higher and weighed on New York listed stocks.

Inflation fears also caused worry among investors as the Federal Reserve voted to hold borrowing costs at 3.75pc.

The S&P 500 shed 91.39 points, or 1.4pc, to close at 6,624.70.The Nasdaq declined 327.11 points, or 1.5pc, to end the day at 22,152.42. The tech-heavy Dow Jones Industrial Average posted its lowest close of 2026 as it fell 768.11 points, or 1.6pc, to 46,225.15.
